      order_month,
      monthly_revenue,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY user_id
        ORDER BY monthly_revenue DESC, order_month ASC
      ) AS rn
    FROM (
      SELECT
        user_id,
        DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS order_month,
        SUM(net_amount) AS monthly_revenue
      FROM orders
      WHERE status = 'completed'
        AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date) = 2024
      GROUP BY user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
    ) monthly_user_revenue
  ) ranked_months
  WHERE rn = 1
) p ON u.user_id = p.user_id
JOIN (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS order_month,
    SUM(net_amount) AS monthly_revenue
  FROM orders
  WHERE status = 'completed'
    AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date) = 2024
  GROUP BY user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
) m ON u.user_id = m.user_id
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name, p.peak_month, p.peak_month_revenue
HAVING COUNT(CASE WHEN m.order_month < p.peak_month THEN 1 END) >= 1
   AND COUNT(CASE WHEN m.order_month > p.peak_month THEN 1 END) >= 1
   AND AVG(CASE WHEN m.order_month > p.peak_month THEN m.monthly_revenue END)
       < AVG(CASE WHEN m.order_month < p.peak_month THEN m.monthly_revenue END)
ORDER BY decline_amount DESC, u.user_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT
  u.user_id,
  u.name,
  p.peak_month,
  p.peak_month_revenue,
  ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN m.order_month < p.peak_month THEN m.monthly_revenue END), 2) AS avg_before_peak,
  ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN m.order_month > p.peak_month THEN m.monthly_revenue END), 2) AS avg_after_peak,
  ROUND(
    AVG(CASE WHEN m.order_month < p.peak_month THEN m.monthly_revenue END)
    - AVG(CASE WHEN m.order_month > p.peak_month THEN m.monthly_revenue END),
    2
  ) AS decline_amount
FROM users u
JOIN (
  SELECT user_id, order_month AS peak_month, monthly_revenue AS peak_month_revenue
  FROM (
    SELECT
      user_id,
      order_month,
      monthly_revenue,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY user_id
        ORDER BY monthly_revenue DESC, order_month ASC
      ) AS rn
    FROM (
      SELECT
        user_id,
        DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS order_month,
        SUM(net_amount) AS monthly_revenue
      FROM orders
      WHERE status = 'completed'
        AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date) = 2024
      GROUP BY user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
    ) monthly_user_revenue
  ) ranked_months
  WHERE rn = 1
) p ON u.user_id = p.user_id
JOIN (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS order_month,
    SUM(net_amount) AS monthly_revenue
  FROM orders
  WHERE status = 'completed'
    AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date) = 2024
  GROUP BY user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
) m ON u.user_id = m.user_id
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name, p.peak_month, p.peak_month_revenue
HAVING COUNT(CASE WHEN m.order_month < p.peak_month THEN 1 END) >= 1
   AND COUNT(CASE WHEN m.order_month > p.peak_month THEN 1 END) >= 1
   AND AVG(CASE WHEN m.order_month > p.peak_month THEN m.monthly_revenue END)
       < AVG(CASE WHEN m.order_month < p.peak_month THEN m.monthly_revenue END)
ORDER BY decline_amount DESC, u.user_id ASC;
explanation: This is a true two-pass problem. The first pass identifies each user's peak month after reducing orders to monthly revenue rows. That peak cannot be read directly from the raw table because it depends on an aggregation and a tie-breaking rule. The second pass reuses the peak-month result to split the monthly history into before-peak and after-peak periods and compare their averages. The hard part is that the same monthly rows are evaluated twice: once to determine the reference point and again to measure decline relative to that reference point.
hints: Compute monthly revenue per user first, then identify the peak month with a deterministic tie-break on the earliest month.
After you know each user's peak month, join back to the monthly revenue rows and use conditional averages for the before-peak and after-peak periods.

title: Users who bought one category, never bought another, but viewed it
description: Return user_id and name for users who satisfy all three conditions: they have at least one completed-order purchase of a product in category_name = 'Category 1', they have never purchased a product in category_name = 'Category 2' in any completed order, and they have at least one view_product event for a product in category_name = 'Category 2'. Use the same user across all three behavioral checks. Order by user_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name
FROM users u
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
  JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
  WHERE o.user_id = u.user_id
    AND o.status = 'completed'
    AND c.category_name = 'Category 1'
)
AND NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
  JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
  WHERE o.user_id = u.user_id
    AND o.status = 'completed'
    AND c.category_name = 'Category 2'
)
AND EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM events e
  JOIN products p ON e.product_id = p.product_id
  JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
  WHERE e.user_id = u.user_id
    AND e.event_name = 'view_product'
    AND c.category_name = 'Category 2'
)
ORDER BY u.user_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name
FROM users u
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
  JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
  WHERE o.user_id = u.user_id
    AND o.status = 'completed'
    AND c.category_name = 'Category 1'
)
AND NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
  JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
  WHERE o.user_id = u.user_id
    AND o.status = 'completed'
    AND c.category_name = 'Category 2'
)
AND EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM events e
  JOIN products p ON e.product_id = p.product_id
  JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
  WHERE e.user_id = u.user_id
    AND e.event_name = 'view_product'
    AND c.category_name = 'Category 2'
)
ORDER BY u.user_id ASC;
explanation: This is a set-difference problem rather than an aggregation problem. The answer depends on whether each user belongs to three overlapping behavioral sets: people who bought from one category, people who never bought from another category, and people who viewed that second category. EXISTS and NOT EXISTS are the right tools because they express those membership tests directly and avoid unnecessary grouping. The hard part is keeping the logic user-centric while evaluating purchases and views from different sources without accidentally collapsing rows or counting the wrong behavior.
hints: Treat the three business conditions as separate user-level membership tests rather than as one large aggregated query.
Use NOT EXISTS for the exclusion rule so a single disqualifying purchase in Category 2 removes the user.

title: User segments with priority-based decision logic
description: Classify every user into exactly one segment using this priority order and no other interpretation: High Value if total completed spend is at least 1000; else Frequent Buyer if completed order count is at least 5; else At Risk if the user has no completed order in the 60 days before the latest order_date in the orders table, meaning last_completed_order_date < reference_date - 60 days and including users with no completed orders at all; else New if signup_date is >= reference_date - 30 days using that same latest order_date as the shared reference point; else Standard. After classification, return segment, user_count, and avg_completed_revenue, where avg_completed_revenue is the average total completed spend per user in the segment rounded to 2 decimal places. Order by user_count descending, then segment ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT segment, COUNT(*) AS user_count, ROUND(AVG(total_completed_spend), 2) AS avg_completed_revenue
FROM (
  SELECT
    u.user_id,
    COALESCE(m.total_completed_spend, 0) AS total_completed_spend,
    CASE
      WHEN COALESCE(m.total_completed_spend, 0) >= 1000 THEN 'High Value'
      WHEN COALESCE(m.completed_order_count, 0) >= 5 THEN 'Frequent Buyer'
      WHEN m.last_completed_order_date IS NULL OR m.last_completed_order_date < r.reference_date - INTERVAL 60 DAY THEN 'At Risk'
      WHEN u.signup_date >= r.reference_date - INTERVAL 30 DAY THEN 'New'
      ELSE 'Standard'
    END AS segment
  FROM users u
  CROSS JOIN (
    SELECT MAX(order_date) AS reference_date
    FROM orders
  ) r
  LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT
      user_id,
      SUM(net_amount) AS total_completed_spend,
      COUNT(*) AS completed_order_count,
      MAX(order_date) AS last_completed_order_date
    FROM orders
    WHERE status = 'completed'
    GROUP BY user_id
  ) m ON u.user_id = m.user_id
) classified_users
GROUP BY segment
ORDER BY user_count DESC, segment ASC
solution_query:
SELECT segment, COUNT(*) AS user_count, ROUND(AVG(total_completed_spend), 2) AS avg_completed_revenue
FROM (
  SELECT
    u.user_id,
    COALESCE(m.total_completed_spend, 0) AS total_completed_spend,
    CASE
      WHEN COALESCE(m.total_completed_spend, 0) >= 1000 THEN 'High Value'
      WHEN COALESCE(m.completed_order_count, 0) >= 5 THEN 'Frequent Buyer'
      WHEN m.last_completed_order_date IS NULL OR m.last_completed_order_date < r.reference_date - INTERVAL 60 DAY THEN 'At Risk'
      WHEN u.signup_date >= r.reference_date - INTERVAL 30 DAY THEN 'New'
      ELSE 'Standard'
    END AS segment
  FROM users u
  CROSS JOIN (
    SELECT MAX(order_date) AS reference_date
    FROM orders
  ) r
  LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT
      user_id,
      SUM(net_amount) AS total_completed_spend,
      COUNT(*) AS completed_order_count,
      MAX(order_date) AS last_completed_order_date
    FROM orders
    WHERE status = 'completed'
    GROUP BY user_id
  ) m ON u.user_id = m.user_id
) classified_users
GROUP BY segment
ORDER BY user_count DESC, segment ASC;
explanation: The important challenge here is not the aggregation itself but the precedence of the classification rules. A user can satisfy multiple conditions at once, so the CASE expression has to encode the exact business priority in the right order. The aggregation comes only after every user has been reduced to one deterministic segment. This is hard because the segmentation logic mixes static user attributes, recency rules, and aggregated purchase behavior, but the final output still requires a single unambiguous bucket per user.
hints: Compute user-level completed-order metrics first, then classify each user once with a CASE expression in the exact priority order given.
Use the latest order_date in the whole orders table as the shared reference date for both the 60-day and 30-day rules.

title: Completed orders contradicted by payment evidence
description: Find orders where order.status is 'completed' but the latest payment status is 'failed' or the order has no payment rows at all. Latest payment means payment_date descending and payment_id descending. Return order_id, user_id, and latest_payment_status, where latest_payment_status should be NULL when no payment exists. Order by order_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT o.order_id, o.user_id, lp.latest_payment_status
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT order_id, status AS latest_payment_status
  FROM (
    SELECT
      order_id,
      status,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY order_id
        ORDER BY payment_date DESC, payment_id DESC
      ) AS rn
    FROM payments
  ) ranked_payments
  WHERE rn = 1
) lp ON o.order_id = lp.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
  AND (lp.latest_payment_status = 'failed' OR lp.latest_payment_status IS NULL)
ORDER BY o.order_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT o.order_id, o.user_id, lp.latest_payment_status
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT order_id, status AS latest_payment_status
  FROM (
    SELECT
      order_id,
      status,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY order_id
        ORDER BY payment_date DESC, payment_id DESC
      ) AS rn
    FROM payments
  ) ranked_payments
  WHERE rn = 1
) lp ON o.order_id = lp.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
  AND (lp.latest_payment_status = 'failed' OR lp.latest_payment_status IS NULL)
ORDER BY o.order_id ASC;
explanation: This is a contradiction-detection query, so the first task is to reduce each order's payment history to a single latest status. A left join is required because the absence of payment rows is itself part of the contradiction rule. Once the latest status is available per order, the final filter is simple: completed orders should not end with a failed payment and should not have missing payment evidence. The hard part is handling the missing-row case correctly without losing those orders during the join.
hints: Derive exactly one latest payment row per order before applying the contradiction filter.
Use a LEFT JOIN so orders with no payments remain visible and can be tested for NULL.

title: Top 2 products per category without window functions
description: Return the top 2 products per category based on total completed-order revenue. Columns: category_id, product_id, product_name, total_revenue. Revenue is the sum of line_amount from completed orders only. Rank products within each category by total_revenue descending, breaking ties on product_id ascending. Do not use window functions. Order by category_id ascending, total_revenue descending, then product_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT a.category_id, a.product_id, a.product_name, a.total_revenue
FROM (
  SELECT p.category_id, p.product_id, p.product_name, SUM(oi.line_amount) AS total_revenue
  FROM products p
  JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY p.category_id, p.product_id, p.product_name
) a
LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT p.category_id, p.product_id, SUM(oi.line_amount) AS total_revenue
  FROM products p
  JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY p.category_id, p.product_id
) b ON a.category_id = b.category_id
 AND (b.total_revenue > a.total_revenue OR (b.total_revenue = a.total_revenue AND b.product_id < a.product_id))
GROUP BY a.category_id, a.product_id, a.product_name, a.total_revenue
HAVING COUNT(b.product_id) < 2
ORDER BY a.category_id ASC, a.total_revenue DESC, a.product_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT a.category_id, a.product_id, a.product_name, a.total_revenue
FROM (
  SELECT p.category_id, p.product_id, p.product_name, SUM(oi.line_amount) AS total_revenue
  FROM products p
  JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY p.category_id, p.product_id, p.product_name
) a
LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT p.category_id, p.product_id, SUM(oi.line_amount) AS total_revenue
  FROM products p
  JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY p.category_id, p.product_id
) b ON a.category_id = b.category_id
 AND (b.total_revenue > a.total_revenue OR (b.total_revenue = a.total_revenue AND b.product_id < a.product_id))
GROUP BY a.category_id, a.product_id, a.product_name, a.total_revenue
HAVING COUNT(b.product_id) < 2
ORDER BY a.category_id ASC, a.total_revenue DESC, a.product_id ASC;
explanation: This question bans the usual ranking shortcut, so the ranking logic has to be simulated manually. The first subquery computes revenue per product within each category. The self-join then compares each candidate product to other products in the same category that outrank it under the exact tie-breaking rule. If fewer than two products outrank the candidate, it belongs in the top 2. The hard part is preserving deterministic ordering without a window function by translating rank position into a count of better competitors.
hints: Start by aggregating completed revenue per product and category in a reusable subquery.
In the self-join, define exactly what it means for one product to outrank another using both revenue and product_id.

title: Users with a timed view-to-cart-to-purchase chain
description: Return user_id and product_id for user-product pairs that satisfy all of these conditions: a view_product event exists, an add_to_cart event for the same user and product occurs in the interval > view_time and <= view_time + 24 hours, and a purchase event for the same user and product occurs in the interval > cart_time and <= cart_time + 48 hours. All three events must belong to the same user-product pair, and the order of events must be strictly view first, then cart, then purchase. Return each qualifying user-product pair once. Order by user_id ascending, then product_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT DISTINCT v.user_id, v.product_id
FROM events v
WHERE v.event_name = 'view_product'
  AND v.product_id IS NOT NULL
  AND EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM events c
    WHERE c.user_id = v.user_id
      AND c.product_id = v.product_id
      AND c.event_name = 'add_to_cart'
      AND c.event_time > v.event_time
      AND c.event_time <= v.event_time + INTERVAL 24 HOUR
      AND EXISTS (
        SELECT 1
        FROM events p
        WHERE p.user_id = c.user_id
          AND p.product_id = c.product_id
          AND p.event_name = 'purchase'
          AND p.event_time > c.event_time
          AND p.event_time <= c.event_time + INTERVAL 48 HOUR
      )
  )
ORDER BY v.user_id ASC, v.product_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT DISTINCT v.user_id, v.product_id
FROM events v
WHERE v.event_name = 'view_product'
  AND v.product_id IS NOT NULL
  AND EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM events c
    WHERE c.user_id = v.user_id
      AND c.product_id = v.product_id
      AND c.event_name = 'add_to_cart'
      AND c.event_time > v.event_time
      AND c.event_time <= v.event_time + INTERVAL 24 HOUR
      AND EXISTS (
        SELECT 1
        FROM events p
        WHERE p.user_id = c.user_id
          AND p.product_id = c.product_id
          AND p.event_name = 'purchase'
          AND p.event_time > c.event_time
          AND p.event_time <= c.event_time + INTERVAL 48 HOUR
      )
  )
ORDER BY v.user_id ASC, v.product_id ASC;
explanation: This is a time-dependent chain problem, so the query must prove that three events happen in the right order and within two separate time limits. EXISTS chains are a natural fit because each stage depends on the existence of a later stage anchored to the same user-product pair. DISTINCT is needed only at the outermost level to collapse multiple qualifying event combinations into one user-product result. The hard part is expressing the chain clearly enough that the time constraints are enforced at the right step rather than across unrelated events.
hints: Anchor the logic on a specific view_product row, then search for later qualifying cart and purchase events for that same user-product pair.
Use nested EXISTS so the purchase check is evaluated relative to a qualifying add_to_cart event, not just relative to the original view.

title: Session-based attribution with conflicting signals
description: For completed orders in 2024, attribute each order's net_amount to a single traffic_source based on session behavior. First-touch is defined as the earliest session traffic_source for the user. Last-touch is defined as the latest session traffic_source from a session that occurred at or before the order_date. If both exist and are different, apply this rule: if the last-touch session occurred within 7 days (inclusive) before the order_date, attribute to last-touch; otherwise attribute to first-touch. If only one exists, use that. If neither exists, exclude the order. Return traffic_source, attributed_orders, attributed_revenue, and revenue_share_pct (percentage of total attributed revenue rounded to 2 decimals). Order by attributed_revenue descending, then traffic_source ascending. Break all ties using session_start ascending with session_id ascending as a tie-breaker.
expected_query:
WITH first_touch AS (
  SELECT user_id, traffic_source, session_start
  FROM (
    SELECT user_id, traffic_source, session_start,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY user_id
        ORDER BY session_start ASC, session_id ASC
      ) AS rn
    FROM sessions
  ) t
  WHERE rn = 1
),
last_touch AS (
  SELECT order_id, traffic_source, session_start
  FROM (
    SELECT o.order_id, s.traffic_source, s.session_start,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY o.order_id
        ORDER BY s.session_start DESC, s.session_id DESC
      ) AS rn
    FROM orders o
    JOIN sessions s ON o.user_id = s.user_id
    WHERE s.session_start <= o.order_date
  ) t
  WHERE rn = 1
),
attributed AS (
  SELECT
    o.order_id,
    o.net_amount,
    CASE
      WHEN lt.traffic_source IS NOT NULL AND ft.traffic_source IS NOT NULL AND lt.traffic_source <> ft.traffic_source THEN
        CASE
          WHEN DATE_DIFF('day', lt.session_start, o.order_date) BETWEEN 0 AND 7 THEN lt.traffic_source
          ELSE ft.traffic_source
        END
      WHEN lt.traffic_source IS NOT NULL THEN lt.traffic_source
      WHEN ft.traffic_source IS NOT NULL THEN ft.traffic_source
      ELSE NULL
    END AS traffic_source
  FROM orders o
  LEFT JOIN first_touch ft ON o.user_id = ft.user_id
  LEFT JOIN last_touch lt ON o.order_id = lt.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
    AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM o.order_date) = 2024
)
SELECT traffic_source,
  COUNT() AS attributed_orders,
  SUM(net_amount) AS attributed_revenue,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(net_amount) / SUM(SUM(net_amount)) OVER (), 2) AS revenue_share_pct
FROM attributed
WHERE traffic_source IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY traffic_source
ORDER BY attributed_revenue DESC, traffic_source ASC
solution_query:
WITH first_touch AS (
  SELECT user_id, traffic_source, session_start
  FROM (
    SELECT user_id, traffic_source, session_start,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY user_id
        ORDER BY session_start ASC, session_id ASC
      ) AS rn
    FROM sessions
  ) t
  WHERE rn = 1
),
last_touch AS (
  SELECT order_id, traffic_source, session_start
  FROM (
    SELECT o.order_id, s.traffic_source, s.session_start,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY o.order_id
        ORDER BY s.session_start DESC, s.session_id DESC
      ) AS rn
    FROM orders o
    JOIN sessions s ON o.user_id = s.user_id
    WHERE s.session_start <= o.order_date
  ) t
  WHERE rn = 1
),
attributed AS (
  SELECT
    o.order_id,
    o.net_amount,
    CASE
      WHEN lt.traffic_source IS NOT NULL AND ft.traffic_source IS NOT NULL AND lt.traffic_source <> ft.traffic_source THEN
        CASE
          WHEN DATE_DIFF('day', lt.session_start, o.order_date) BETWEEN 0 AND 7 THEN lt.traffic_source
          ELSE ft.traffic_source
        END
      WHEN lt.traffic_source IS NOT NULL THEN lt.traffic_source
      WHEN ft.traffic_source IS NOT NULL THEN ft.traffic_source
      ELSE NULL
    END AS traffic_source
  FROM orders o
  LEFT JOIN first_touch ft ON o.user_id = ft.user_id
  LEFT JOIN last_touch lt ON o.order_id = lt.order_id
  WHERE o.status = 'completed'
    AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM o.order_date) = 2024
)
SELECT traffic_source,
  COUNT() AS attributed_orders,
  SUM(net_amount) AS attributed_revenue,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(net_amount) / SUM(SUM(net_amount)) OVER (), 2) AS revenue_share_pct
FROM attributed
WHERE traffic_source IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY traffic_source
